subject: Two-handed weapons (greatswords, greataxes, mauls, greatclubs)
method: full human/LLM read of transcript 065 (not the heuristic)
note: All 33 tiers below were confirmed to match the stored tierlists.json exactly. The 'why' field is new content. The highest-damage weapon category; rated by power when acquired. Greatswords/mauls roll 2d6 (highest, most consistent base damage); Cleave and Prepare/Brace are prized weapon actions. (Githyanki Greatsword here is the looted psychic-damage version, not the plain +1.)

Blooded Greataxe.why: A +1 greataxe with +1d4 when at/below half HP, buyable from a merchant with no fights — usually the first real two-handed upgrade you get; works either as a plain +1 axe or a damage-boosted weapon if you start combat below half.

Everburn Blade.why: A no-enhancement greatsword with +1d4 fire on every hit, looted on the nautiloid — the first magical weapon and a strict upgrade over a starting greataxe; a big damage bump unless you meticulously fire-dip every weapon (then it's merely redundant).

Githyanki Greatsword.why: The looted (githyanki-patrol) version: a +1 greatsword that deals +1d4 psychic when wielded by a gith (or via Disguise Self), making it a strict upgrade over the Blooded Greataxe and Everburn Blade — the best weapon you have at that point.

Soulbreaker Greatsword.why: A +1 greatsword with gith psychic bonus, +2 initiative, and a once/rest action that stuns for two turns — best two-handed weapon in Act 1 and for many characters most of the game; the initiative patches a paladin's weakness and helps land the stun first.

Sword of Justice.why: A +1 greatsword granting Shield of Faith (+2 AC) as a bonus action once/rest — and since two-handed users rarely need concentration, it's effectively a permanent +2 AC; the defensive counterpart to the Blooded Greataxe, available very early.

Balduran's Giantslayer.why: A legendary +3 greatsword that DOUBLES your strength modifier to damage (≈+16 with a Cloud Giant elixir), advantage vs large+ enemies, a free Enlarge, and a prone action — the best, highest-damage two-handed weapon in the game for single targets.

Hellfire Greataxe.why: A +2 greataxe with +1d6 fire, Heat stacking, and a once/rest Hellflame Cleave that leaves a 6d6/turn Hellfire field — the best AOE-damage weapon in the game (and, being coded as a cantrip, a sorcerer can even Quicken it).

Hamarhraft.why: A no-enhancement maul dealing 1d4 thunder AOE whenever you jump, with no once-per-turn limit — a monk (Step of the Wind) or anyone spamming the Fly action can trigger dozens of shockwaves a turn for record-setting damage; even casually, it turns a Flying potion into a bomb.

Jorgoral's Greatsword.why: A +1 greatsword with a once/rest Colossal Onslaught — a fireball-sized line attack (weapon damage, half on a miss) that gives single-target melee characters a strong AOE option; worth swapping in for crowds.

Sussur Greatsword.why: A +1 greatsword forcing a DC12 con save or Silenced for two turns on hit; great for locking down enemy spellcasters and bosses with non-spell legendary actions — the two-handed pick of the Sussur flower weapons (take the dagger if you can't use this).

Svartlebee's Woundseeker.why: A +1 greatsword granting +1d4 to hit against enemies that have already taken damage (easy to set up) — accuracy is hugely valuable for Great Weapon Master users; great for high-AC, high-HP boss fights, available in Act 1.

Punch-Drunk Bastard.why: A +1 greatclub (with Cleave) that, while drunk, grants advantage on all attacks and a 1d4 thunder AOE shockwave on every hit — combined with Cleave it instantly stacks max reverberation, one of the best ways to apply it; enables a drunk-reverb build.

Silver Sword of the Astral Plane.why: A legendary +3 greatsword with gith psychic bonus, advantage on mental saves (for gith), and the Soulbreaker two-turn stun action; excellent and accurate, but more of a defensive endgame pick than the higher-damage Giantslayer/Hellfire Greataxe.

Sword of Chaos.why: A +2 greatsword with +1d4 necrotic that heals 1d6 on every hit — strong sustain, especially on AOE/cleave attacks and damage-halving barbarians, and it triggers on-heal item synergies; a solid default and enabler of healing builds.

Greataxe.why: Non-magical d12 with a great chassis (Cleave, plus Prepare to double your strength mod to damage), and a barbarian's starting weapon — the highest-damage weapon you can begin the game with, very useful on the nautiloid before upgrades.

Exterminator's Axe.why: A no-enhancement greataxe with +1d6 fire vs plants, myconids, and small enemies (which includes many Act 1 goblins/underdark foes), and the bonus even stacks with fire-dipping — a worthwhile swap-in for those common encounters.

Corpsegrinder.why: A +2 maul with +1d4 thunder and a once/rest 10ft AOE attack that shoves enemies (by your shove distance) — a solid weapon with a relevant damage type and a mini-fireball, though most prefer the Soulbreaker when it's available.

Greatsword.why: Non-magical 2d6 (highest, most consistent base damage) with Cleave, Lacerate, and a bonus-action Pummel Strike (daze) — a great chassis, but you can't start with one, so you'll just upgrade to a magical weapon ASAP.

Maul.why: Non-magical 2d6 with one of the best weapon-action sets (Tenacity, Concussive Smash, and Back Breaker prone); an excellent chassis you'll only use briefly since you don't start with it.

Doom Hammer.why: A no-enhancement maul applying Bone Chill (no healing; disadvantage for undead) on hit; situationally good against healers/undead, but it hits less and does less than other great weapons, and an Arrow of Ilmater or the cantrip applies the effect with less opportunity cost.

Argument Solver.why: A +1 greatclub (with the maul's full weapon actions) dealing +proficiency poison and creating poison clouds (DC11 con save) on hit; the low damage and low DC make it weak now, but it's a cute base for a poison-cloud themed build.

Rat Bat.why: A +1 greatclub with advantage vs beasts and an undocumented +1d6 piercing; decent damage for a simple-weapon-only two-handed user, but mostly notable for being absurdly buggy (record damage) below honour mode — niche on honour.

Sethan.why: A +2 greataxe granting a level-6 Spiritual Weapon (greataxe) once/rest plus Reduce; a moderately useful bonus-action spell you might swap in when desperate for long-rest resources, but usually a more powerful weapon is better.

Greatclub.why: Non-magical d8 simple weapon with okay actions (Tenacity, Concussive Smash), but simple-weapon users do better with a quarterstaff and martial users with a higher-damage weapon — lowest damage in the category.

+1 Greataxe.why: A generic +1 greataxe; early merchants and encounters provide strictly-better or equal magical greataxes, so there's no reason to buy it.

+1 Greatclub.why: A generic +1 greatclub; outclassed by better options available right from the start of the game.

+1 Greatsword.why: A generic +1 greatsword; the Everburn Blade and other early magical greatswords are strictly better, so it's never needed.

+1 Maul.why: A generic +1 maul; better magical two-handed weapons are available from the very start, so there's no reason to use it.

Very Heavy Greataxe.why: A +1 greataxe whose 'special' action is just Cleave plus +1d6 and a self-debuff (fall-prone-when-hit) — a strictly worse version of an action every greataxe already has; funny name, terrible weapon.

Defender Greataxe.why: A +2 greataxe that can drop to +1 for +1 AC/saves; a defensive bonus is the wrong fit for a two-handed damage weapon (use a shield instead), and the save bonus isn't active at combat start when it matters most.

+2 Greataxe.why: A generic +2 greataxe found only at the very end; by then you have far better weapons, so it's pointless.

Foebreaker.why: A +2 maul that ignores bludgeoning resistance; resistance is better bypassed with a different damage type or damage riders, and there's no encounter where this beats your other weapons — safely ignored.

The Undead Bane.why: A +1 greataxe with +1d6 vs fiends/undead and a once/rest 2d6 + Bane action; even in its target encounters the bonus is weaker than generically powerful weapons (e.g. the Soulbreaker's stun), so the specialist loses out.
